title: Podman 2.0.3 could not start a detached container on Jenkins slave
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
I try to start podman **rootless** on Jenkins slave (vm). When I use `podman run -d ..`, it crashes at startup with **permission denied**.
But when I manually login on Jenkins slave via ssh with the same user as Jenkins slave works, and execute the same command from the same directory, it works.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Write Jenkins pipeline
```groovy
node('linux') {
    sh "podman run --detach --name dntjzy9t_container private_registry/container:latest /bin/bash"
}
```
2. Run Jenkins job
3. Open build log and view error
```text
+ podman run --detach --name dntjzy9t_container private_registry/container:latest /bin/bash
Error: container_linux.go:367: starting container process caused: process_linux.go:459: container init caused: process_linux.go:382: setting rlimits for ready process caused: error setting rlimit type 7: operation not permitted: OCI runtime permission denied error
```

**Describe the results you received:**
Podman not started a new container

**Describe the results you expected:**
Podman should start a new container in detached mode

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Jenkins home is the same as user home 

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.0.3
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.13.4
Built:        Thu Jan  1 03:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
VM (OpenStack)

`cat ~/.config/containers/containers.conf`
```
[containers]

 default_ulimits = [
   "nofile=262144:262144",
 ]
```

comments.body:
This looks like you are attempting to set the ulimits for NOFILE to higher level then is allowed for  the user to set.  I think when you login directly pam_limits is being set, but when you run it in jenkens the process is getting different limits.

@giuseppe @mheon WDYT?

I don't see the limits being explicitly say anywhere, unless they were in
containers.conf?

On Sat, Jul 25, 2020, 07:16 Daniel J Walsh <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> This looks like you are attempting to set the ulimits for NOFILE to higher
> level then is allowed for the user to set. I think when you login directly
> pam_limits is being set, but when you run it in jenkens the process is
> getting different limits.
>
> @giuseppe <https://github.com/giuseppe> @mheon <https://github.com/mheon>
> WDYT?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/7082#issuecomment-663843623>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCC7PRQXXQKF4NI2KN3R5K5KNANCNFSM4PHDC3NQ>
> .
>

Ahhh, missed the bit at the end where they were. Agree with Dan, probably
set too high, higher than the current system allows.

On Sat, Jul 25, 2020, 10:28 Matthew Heon <matthew.heon@gmail.com> wrote:

> I don't see the limits being explicitly say anywhere, unless they were in
> containers.conf?
>
> On Sat, Jul 25, 2020, 07:16 Daniel J Walsh <notifications@github.com>
> wrote:
>
>> This looks like you are attempting to set the ulimits for NOFILE to
>> higher level then is allowed for the user to set. I think when you login
>> directly pam_limits is being set, but when you run it in jenkens the
>> process is getting different limits.
>>
>> @giuseppe <https://github.com/giuseppe> @mheon <https://github.com/mheon>
>> WDYT?
>>
>> —
>> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
>> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
>> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/7082#issuecomment-663843623>,
>> or unsubscribe
>> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCC7PRQXXQKF4NI2KN3R5K5KNANCNFSM4PHDC3NQ>
>> .
>>
>

Limits from `/etc/security` directory. limits.conf has no uncommented lines
```bash
[root@pc /etc/security]# ls limits.d/
90-nofiles.conf
[root@pc /etc/security]# cat limits.d/90-nofiles.conf 
*          soft    nofile     262144
*          hard    nofile     262144
```
File `/usr/share/containers/containers.conf` was not modified after installation. File `/etc/containers/containers.conf` not exists.


I run `ulimit -a`:
From **ssh** terminal as Jenkins user
```bash
[jenkins@pc ~]$ ulimit -a
core file size          (blocks, -c) unlimited
data seg size           (kbytes, -d) unlimited
scheduling priority             (-e) 0
file size               (blocks, -f) unlimited
pending signals                 (-i) 256904
max locked memory       (kbytes, -l) 16384
max memory size         (kbytes, -m) unlimited
open files                      (-n) 262144
pipe size            (512 bytes, -p) 8
POSIX message queues     (bytes, -q) 819200
real-time priority              (-r) 0
stack size              (kbytes, -s) 8192
cpu time               (seconds, -t) unlimited
max user processes              (-u) 256904
virtual memory          (kbytes, -v) unlimited
file locks                      (-x) unlimited
```
Next run from **Jenkins pipeine**
```groovy
node('linux') {
    sh "ulimit -a"  
}
```
and output
```bash
core file size          (blocks, -c) unlimited
data seg size           (kbytes, -d) unlimited
scheduling priority             (-e) 0
file size               (blocks, -f) unlimited
pending signals                 (-i) 256904
max locked memory       (kbytes, -l) 16384
max memory size         (kbytes, -m) unlimited
open files                      (-n) 4096 ## <- this is **incorrect** limit
pipe size            (512 bytes, -p) 8
POSIX message queues     (bytes, -q) 819200
real-time priority              (-r) 0
stack size              (kbytes, -s) 8192
cpu time               (seconds, -t) unlimited
max user processes              (-u) 256904
virtual memory          (kbytes, -v) unlimited
file locks                      (-x) unlimited
```
Ok. I added limit to `/etc/systemd/system/jenkins-agent.service` and restart service
```bash
[root@pc /etc/systemd/system]# cat jenkins-agent.service 
[Service]
...
LimitNOFILE=262144
...
```
And execute pipeline once again
```bash
+ ulimit -a
core file size          (blocks, -c) unlimited
data seg size           (kbytes, -d) unlimited
scheduling priority             (-e) 0
file size               (blocks, -f) unlimited
pending signals                 (-i) 256904
max locked memory       (kbytes, -l) 16384
max memory size         (kbytes, -m) unlimited
open files                      (-n) 262144 ## <- now correct limit
pipe size            (512 bytes, -p) 8
POSIX message queues     (bytes, -q) 819200
real-time priority              (-r) 0
stack size              (kbytes, -s) 8192
cpu time               (seconds, -t) unlimited
max user processes              (-u) 256904
virtual memory          (kbytes, -v) unlimited
file locks                      (-x) unlimited
```
Then verify limits inside container (it started!) from Jenkins pipeline
```bash
+ podman run --name dntjzy9t_container private_registry/container:latest ulimit -a
core file size          (blocks, -c) unlimited
data seg size           (kbytes, -d) unlimited
scheduling priority             (-e) 0
file size               (blocks, -f) unlimited
pending signals                 (-i) 256904
max locked memory       (kbytes, -l) 16384
max memory size         (kbytes, -m) unlimited
open files                      (-n) 262144 ## <- limits also passed
pipe size            (512 bytes, -p) 8
POSIX message queues     (bytes, -q) 819200
real-time priority              (-r) 0
stack size              (kbytes, -s) 8192
cpu time               (seconds, -t) unlimited
max user processes              (-u) 32768
virtual memory          (kbytes, -v) unlimited
file locks                      (-x) unlimited
```
Good. And the last check from pipeline:
```groovy
node('linux') {
    sh "podman run -d --name dntjzy9t_container private_registry/container:latest tail -f /dev/null"
    sh "podman exec -i dntjzy9t_container ls"
    sh "podman rm -f dntjzy9t_container"
}
```
and it works!
```bash
+ podman run -d --name dntjzy9t_container private_registry/container:latest tail -f /dev/null
39c44844ab203cbb0df29d2241100354803b7b453ed62f62d2f9d595848c60eb
[Pipeline] sh
+ podman exec -i dntjzy9t_container ls
bin
boot
...
[Pipeline] sh
+ podman rm -f dntjzy9t_container
39c44844ab203cbb0df29d2241100354803b7b453ed62f62d2f9d595848c60eb
```
Ok. But how about accessing such container from **ssh** terminal?
1. From pipeline I started a container
```groovy
node('linux') {
    sh "podman run --detach --name dntjzy9t_container private_registry/container:latest tail -f /dev/null"
}
```
2. login via **ssh** to agent host as Jenkins user and try access to container
```bash
[jenkins@pc ~]$ podman exec -i dntjzy9t_container ls
Error: exec failed: container_linux.go:367: starting container process caused: transport endpoint is not connected: OCI runtime error
```
Maybe error because container started as child process from linux service (via systemd)?
Thank You to help me solving this issue in my configuration.

Yes when you login to a user session via ssh you are executing pam_limits which reads /etc/security/limits.conf and sets it up for the user account.

If you login to the system as root and su to the user, or launch the user process via systemd, then the pam_limits is not executed and the session will have different limits.
